metadata.summary: Manage pytest-django fixtures: db vs transactional_db semantics, fixture scopes, conftest organisation, API client fixtures, and composed fixture patterns.

content.title: Django pytest Fixtures
content.summary: pytest-django provides database fixtures with distinct transaction semantics (db, transactional_db, django_db_reset_sequences). Choosing the correct one and composing fixtures explicitly in conftest.py is the foundation of a reliable, fast, and maintainable Django test suite.

why.p: pytest-django bridges pytest's explicit fixture system with Django's ORM and request handling. Compared to Django's native TestCase setUp/tearDown, fixtures declare dependencies in function signatures, making tests self-documenting and reducing hidden coupling. The fixture scope system (function, class, module, session) lets expensive setup operations be amortized across multiple tests without sacrificing isolation.

list.item: Writing unit or integration tests for Django models, services, and utilities.
Setting up DRF API clients for endpoint tests.
Sharing test data across a test module or session without re-creating it for each test.
Replacing Django TestCase setUp/tearDown with composable, reusable fixtures.
Debugging flaky tests caused by transaction semantics or fixture scope mismatches.
Pure unit tests of helpers or pure functions with no Django dependency — plain pytest (no pytest-django) is faster and clearer.
One-off scripts or ad-hoc manage.py shell exploration — fixture infrastructure overhead is not justified.
Tests that must run inside manage.py test (legacy CI gates with Django runner) until that constraint is removed.

section.name: database-fixture-semantics
section.p: pytest-django provides several database fixtures with different transaction semantics. Choosing the wrong one causes flaky TransactionManagementError exceptions or persistent state leaking between tests.

rule.statement: Any test that accesses the database MUST be marked with @pytest.mark.django_db. Fixtures that create database objects MUST explicitly request the 'db' fixture. Use transactional_db only when the test asserts on transaction commit/rollback semantics, post-commit hooks, signals, or select_for_update. Default to 'db' for all other cases.
rule.rationale: The db fixture wraps each test in a transaction and rolls back after the test, ensuring isolation. Omitting the marker causes "no database access allowed" errors. Mismatching db and transactional_db creates flaky tests that depend on transaction semantics but are not marked for it. transactional_db commits real transactions and requires explicit cleanup.

section.name: fixture-types-table
section.p: Fixture type selection guide: - db: basic database access with auto-rollback (default for all tests). - transactional_db: no auto-rollback; use when testing transaction.atomic, post-commit hooks, select_for_update. - django_db_reset_sequences: resets auto-increment IDs; use when testing ID generation or sequential ordering. - django_db_serialized_rollback: multi-database setups with serialized fixture rollback.

example.title: Correct database fixture usage
Missing db fixture or marker
example.note: Either error causes the test to fail with database access errors or test isolation violations.

section.name: fixture-scope
section.p: Fixture scope controls how long a fixture lives and when it is torn down. Mismatching scope and mutation semantics is a leading cause of flaky tests.

rule.statement: Use function scope (default) for mutable data. Use module scope for shared test data within a file. Use session scope only for immutable, expensive-to-create data (e.g., static categories loaded once). Session-scoped fixtures MUST be immutable — creating copies for each test unless explicitly intending shared state. Session-scoped DB fixtures combined with function-scoped Factory Boy fixtures work only if every write rolls back.
rule.rationale: Function-scoped fixtures guarantee test isolation: each test gets a fresh copy. Session-scoped fixtures amortize one-time expensive operations but must be read-only — mutations accumulate across tests and create order dependencies. Scope mismatch is hard to debug because failures appear random.

section.name: scope-table
section.p: Scope selection: - function: mutable data, default, safest. - class: shared within test class, rare. - module: shared within test file, for read-only reference data. - session: immutable one-time expensive setup (loading categories, static data).

example.title: Session-scoped immutable fixture
example.note: loaddata in session scope runs once; sample_products in module scope creates once per file. Both are read-only to downstream tests.

section.name: api-client-fixtures
section.p: DRF API tests need authenticated client fixtures. Create them in conftest.py with force_authenticate for speed and correctness.

rule.statement: Create api_client, authenticated_client, and admin_client fixtures in root conftest.py. Use APIClient.force_authenticate(user=...) instead of login flows. Use yield to clear authentication after each test. Never use credentials() for test authentication unless the test is specifically about the token exchange flow.
rule.rationale: force_authenticate bypasses the authentication middleware, making API tests 10-100x faster and independent of the authentication backend. yield ensures cleanup (force_authenticate(user=None)) even when tests fail. credentials() tests the token pipeline, not the business logic; mix them only when the test is about authentication itself.

example.title: API client fixtures

section.name: composed-fixtures
section.p: Compose fixtures explicitly by requesting multiple fixture parameters. This makes dependencies visible in the function signature and avoids hidden state coupling.

rule.statement: Compose fixtures by declaring them as function parameters. Use pytest_plugins to modularize fixtures across subdirectories. Avoid class attributes for fixture state — use yield fixtures with explicit teardown. Each conftest.py only contains fixtures relevant to its directory and below.
rule.rationale: Explicit composition makes test dependencies visible and searchable. pytest_plugins lets domain-specific fixtures live near domain-specific tests without polluting the root conftest. Class attributes for state are reset between test methods but not between fixture invocations, causing subtle leaks.

example.title: Composed fixture pattern

section.name: conftest-structure
section.p: A well-structured conftest.py hierarchy keeps shared fixtures centrally defined and directory-specific fixtures close to the tests that use them.

rule.statement: Root conftest.py holds fixtures shared across all tests: API clients, factory registration, global settings overrides. Each subdirectory (unit/, integration/, e2e/) MAY have its own conftest.py with directory-specific fixtures. Use pytest_plugins inside a conftest.py to load modular fixture files. Avoid duplicating fixtures across conftest files.
rule.rationale: The conftest hierarchy follows pytest's natural file system scoping: root conftest is visible everywhere, subdirectory conftest is visible only to that subtree. Keeping shared fixtures in one root file prevents drift. pytest_plugins breaks a large conftest into domain files without losing automatic loading.

example.title: Root conftest.py template

section.name: test-settings
section.p: A dedicated test settings module makes tests faster and prevents side effects in the real environment.

rule.statement: Create config/settings/test.py inheriting from base settings. Override: DATABASES to in-memory SQLite (or Postgres in CI via test_postgres.py), PASSWORD_HASHERS to MD5PasswordHasher, EMAIL_BACKEND to locmem, CELERY_TASK_ALWAYS_EAGER=True, CACHES to locmem. Set MEDIA_ROOT via autouse fixture (not settings file) so each test run gets a fresh temp dir.
rule.rationale: In-memory SQLite is faster than Postgres for unit tests. MD5PasswordHasher is ~200x faster than bcrypt. locmem email captures outgoing mail for assertions. Eager Celery makes tasks run synchronously, enabling assertions immediately after the call. autouse fixture for MEDIA_ROOT ensures tmp_path cleanup per test.

example.title: Test settings module

section.name: common-pitfalls
section.p: Known failure modes in pytest-django fixture usage and how to avoid them.

antipattern.description: db vs transactional_db confusion: using db for a test that asserts on commit semantics, post-commit hooks, or signals.
antipattern.reason: The db fixture wraps the test in a savepoint; post-commit hooks and signals that fire on real commits never execute. Use transactional_db for any test involving transaction.on_commit, signals, post_save, or select_for_update.

antipattern.description: Session-scoped mutable fixtures: creating a session-scoped factory instance that different tests modify.
antipattern.reason: Session-scoped fixtures are shared across all tests in the session. Any mutation accumulates and creates order-dependent failures. Session scope is only safe for truly immutable data (read-only reference tables, static config).

antipattern.description: pytest-xdist + --reuse-db racing on migrations on first run.
antipattern.reason: Multiple workers try to create the database simultaneously. Use --create-db on cold start (first run or after schema changes), then --reuse-db for subsequent runs.

antipattern.description: override_settings inside a fixture without using the pytest-django settings fixture.
antipattern.reason: override_settings as a context manager or decorator does not cascade cleanly into session-scoped fixtures. Use the settings fixture from pytest-django; it is properly scoped and cleaned up.

antipattern.description: MEDIA_ROOT not overridden in test settings, causing tests to write files to the real disk.
antipattern.reason: Tests that create uploads pollute the real MEDIA_ROOT and each other. Always override MEDIA_ROOT via an autouse fixture using tmp_path so each test gets a clean directory that is deleted after the test.

section.name: performance-fixtures
section.p: Performance optimization in fixtures: parallel execution, database reuse, and faster settings.

rule.statement: Run tests in parallel with pytest-xdist: pytest -n auto. Use --reuse-db to skip migrations on subsequent local runs (use --create-db on first run or after schema change). Session-scope immutable data fixtures. Profile test duration with pytest --durations=10. Mark slow tests with @pytest.mark.slow and run them in a separate CI job.
rule.rationale: Parallel execution scales with CPU cores (2-4x speedup on quad-core). Database reuse skips migration cost (20-50% savings on suites with many apps). Profiling reveals unexpected slowdowns. Slow markers let CI run fast tests on every PR and slow tests on merge only.

example.title: Performance-optimised fixtures and CLI

section.name: directory-structure
section.p: Recommended test directory layout for Django projects using pytest.

rule.statement: Organize tests as: tests/conftest.py (root fixtures), tests/factories/ (Factory Boy classes), tests/fixtures/ (complex composed fixtures), tests/unit/ (unit tests, no DB), tests/integration/ (DB + API), tests/e2e/ (end-to-end). Each subdirectory MAY have its own conftest.py for directory-specific fixtures.
rule.rationale: This structure scales from small projects to monorepos. Unit tests run fastest (no DB). Integration tests share factories. conftest inheritance (root → unit/ → module) manages fixture scope cleanly. Developers know immediately where a test belongs and what setup it requires.

example.title: Recommended test directory structure
example.code: tests/ ├── conftest.py # Root fixtures (shared across all tests) ├── factories/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── users.py # UserFactory, ProfileFactory │ └── orders.py # OrderFactory, OrderItemFactory ├── fixtures/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── api.py # API client fixtures │ └── data.py # Data fixtures ├── unit/ │ ├── conftest.py # Unit-specific fixtures │ ├── test_models.py │ ├── test_services.py │ └── test_validators.py ├── integration/ │ ├── conftest.py # Integration-specific fixtures │ ├── test_api.py │ └── test_views.py └── e2e/ ├── conftest.py └── test_flows.py

checklist.check: All tests touching the database are marked with @pytest.mark.django_db
Fixtures that create database objects request the 'db' fixture explicitly
transactional_db used only for tests asserting on commits, signals, or select_for_update
API client fixtures use force_authenticate, not credentials()
Yield used in client fixtures so authentication is cleared after each test
Session-scoped fixtures contain only immutable data
MEDIA_ROOT overridden via autouse fixture using tmp_path
Test settings module uses MD5PasswordHasher, locmem email, eager Celery
Root conftest.py contains shared fixtures; subdirectory conftest.py contains local fixtures
pytest -n auto runs tests in parallel without failures
